evidence: The config package exposes mutable package-level process state (`BasePath`, `Architecture`, `DebugMode`, `Ready`, `DockerDir`) in `goseg/config/bootstrap.go`, while a higher-level context abstraction (`RuntimeContext` and `RuntimeContextSnapshot`) exists in `goseg/config/config.go` but is not consistently used by orchestration constructors.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go` has a global-seeking constructor (`llamaRuntimeFromGlobals`) that reads `config.DockerDir`/`config.UrbitsConfig` directly, alongside other constructors that use dependency-injected runtime fields.
The startram backup area adds thin local pass-through wrappers (`encryptFile`/`decryptFile`) instead of owning one clear API boundary.

Startup orchestration is configured through package-level constructor seams plus direct concrete assignments, but callers still operate at a narrow edge of the same package (`goseg/startup_orchestrator.go`) instead of through stable interfaces.

The startup boundary mixes cross-cutting concerns (auth/router/system/exporter/importer/broadcast/dockers) in a single assembly path, making unit seams brittle and coupling test setup to internal details (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`, `goseg/startup_orchestrator.go`).

Initialization flow in `newStartupOrchestrator` mixes fatal and non-fatal initialization behaviors across sibling subsystem bootstraps, producing non-uniform failure contracts within one orchestrator phase.

That ambiguity is visible in how some bootstraps append errors while others are invoked without error handling, and one path logs and continues even when dependency setup is critical for later systems (`goseg/startup_orchestrator.go`, `goseg/main.go`).

`WriteWgConfWithRuntime` returns a wrapped error on the nominal success path of writing config, because it wraps the return value of `writeWGConfigArtifactWithRuntime` with `%w` unconditionally after deciding an update is needed.
This turns successful config reconciliation into an error path and distorts higher-level caller behavior (`goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard_test.go`).

Websocket session lifecycle and dependency wiring use multiple mutable globals/indirection layers (`auth`, `structs`, `logger`, `runtime` adapters), but some layers intentionally return success while silently skipping required runtime work.

This creates hidden no-op paths and object-identity drift that weakens seam consistency across packages and makes initialization and state-handling behavior non-deterministic under partial configuration.

findings.summary: WireGuard config reconciliation currently returns a wrapped error even when writing succeeds.
evidence: In `WriteWgConfWithRuntime`, after detecting config drift and calling `writeWGConfigArtifactWithRuntime`, the function returns `fmt.Errorf("failed to update wireguard config: %w", ...)` instead of returning success when the write helper returns `nil`.
findings.suggestion: Return nil on successful write, and only wrap/propagate an error when write fails. Optionally guard with an explicit `if err := writeWGConfigArtifactWithRuntime(...); err != nil { return fmt.Errorf(..., err) } return nil` branch.

findings.summary: Subsystem initialization in startup orchestration lacks a consistent success/failure contract.
evidence: Some initializer steps are invoked without capturing return values while others are collected in an error list and can fail startup; this makes behavior order-dependent and harder to reason about.
At least one initializer logs/continues on failure while other failures are treated as hard errors, which undermines explicit subsystem contract clarity.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single initialization contract enum or interface (`required`, `optional`, `disabled`) and enforce one failure policy per subsystem in one place so all startup paths are explicit, testable, and deterministic.

findings.summary: Startup assembly remains tightly coupled to concrete orchestration internals, weakening abstraction boundaries.
evidence: The startup module binds multiple runtime/bootstrap functions through direct wiring and package-level seams instead of explicit subsystem interfaces, reducing substitutability and making composition logic depend on concrete init details.
This tight coupling is visible in the same boundary that also needs special-case handling for failure modes (another sign of insufficient abstraction boundary shaping).
findings.suggestion: Define explicit subsystem interface contracts for each startup concern (router/auth/importer/etc.) and inject implementations through dependency objects; keep seam functions minimal and declarative.

findings.summary: Global mutable config state is treated as the primary seam despite explicit runtime-context abstractions, creating mixed ownership and inconsistent boundaries.
evidence: `goseg/config/bootstrap.go` declares package-level mutable configuration variables (`BasePath`, `Architecture`, `DebugMode`, `Ready`, `DockerDir`) and updates process state during initialization.
`goseg/config/config.go` introduces `RuntimeContext` and `RuntimeContextSnapshot` specifically to pass execution context explicitly.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go:20` still reads `config.DockerDir` directly inside `llamaRuntimeFromGlobals`, bypassing that explicit context channel.
findings.suggestion: Complete the migration to explicit context passing: make orchestration constructors accept a bounded runtime context interface/struct and remove `llamaRuntimeFromGlobals` global reads so initialization flows through dependency injection only.

findings.summary: Orchestration container factory pattern is inconsistent: one runtime is built from globals while peers are built from composable op groups, reducing clarity of architectural contracts.
evidence: `llamaRuntimeFromGlobals` (in `container_bridge.go`) depends on package globals and names itself as the outlier against other factories that take a `dockerRuntime` aggregate.
The coexistence of this factory style with the `netdataRuntimeFromDocker`/`minioRuntimeFromDocker` patterns is not signaled by package-level naming or interface boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Align all container runtime constructors behind a single composition style (prefer injected runtime context + interfaces) and delete global-seeking constructor variants.

findings.summary: Startram adds redundant crypto passthrough functions instead of keeping a single domain boundary, increasing API confusion and package sprawl.
evidence: `goseg/startram/backup_crypto.go` only wraps `backup.EncryptFile` and `backup.DecryptFile` without transformation.
Tests in `backup_runtime_test.go` validate these local wrappers instead of importing the underlying backup API directly.
Other backup restore logic in `backup_restore_runtime.go` already talks to the backup package directly for persistence and fetch operations, so the wrapper layer adds no coherent policy.
findings.suggestion: Use the `backup` package API directly in startram callers/tests and remove `backup_crypto.go`, keeping one clear cryptographic API owner.

findings.summary: Redundant exported/unexported duplicate symbol pair in container config adds avoidable public API noise and signals unstable package ownership choices.
evidence: `ContainerConfigForType` and `containerConfigForType` are functionally identical, with the unexported version only delegating to the exported one.
Tests and callsites gain no benefit from having both, yet maintainers must reason about two possible entry points.
findings.suggestion: Remove the unnecessary alias and keep a single canonical constructor name, updating any internal references accordingly.

findings.summary: Protocol and error contracts lack explicit compatibility policy in the contract-bearing source files.
evidence: README.md mandates contract governance via runtime contract gate checks, but `actions.go` only defines enum and payload rules without any contract lifecycle metadata (no versioning, no deprecation/removal signals, no compatibility mode flags).

`errors.go` exposes `APIConnectionErrorMessage` as a public string contract while leaving masking behavior in a private helper, creating a message-based contract instead of a typed/runtime-checked one.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit contract governance metadata in-code (for example, a contract descriptor struct with fields like `introducedIn`, `deprecatedIn`, `removedIn`, `compatibility`) for upload actions and startram error classes, and export a typed contract API (plus compatibility-check helpers) that can be consumed by tests/gates instead of relying on raw strings.

findings.summary: GetMuConn silently creates detached MuConn wrappers instead of returning managed session state
evidence: `GetMuConn` says it should find or create a MuConn, but the creation path does not insert the new value into the manager maps.
Because this returned wrapper can differ from later auth-map wrappers, websocket operations can run on separate mutex/connection-state objects instead of a single managed session object.
findings.suggestion: Refactor `GetMuConn` to return a managed object from the same lifecycle map (or make it explicit as an unmanaged session-only constructor); normalize callers to always use the same MuConn instance for read/write and activity tracking so locking/active state is coherent.

findings.summary: Adapter builders turn missing copy-to-volume dependencies into silent no-ops
evidence: `runtime_seams.go` installs `CopyFileToVolumeFn` closures that return `nil` when `rt.commandOps.CopyFileToVolumeFn` is nil.
Downstream netdata/minio write paths call the copied artifact path without explicit guard, so artifact generation can report success while key side effects are skipped.
The current contract leaks dependency-health expectations into two adapters instead of failing fast at construction.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared adapter helper that requires `CopyFileToVolumeFn` and returns an explicit error when absent; remove silent fall-through in these wrappers and fail adapter initialization early.

findings.summary: One-shot logger init makes startup failures sticky and unrecoverable
evidence: `logger.Initialize` uses `loggerInitOnce.Do(...)` and stores `loggerInitErr` globally.
The test suite needs to mutate internal state (`loggerInitOnce`, `loggerInitErr`) to re-run init, indicating initialization cannot recover naturally once a failure path is hit.
A transient environment issue can permanently block initialization until process restart.
findings.suggestion: Replace the always-permanent once-gate with a retriable init state machine (or explicit reset/reconfigure entrypoint) so fatal-but-transient setup issues can be retried after conditions are corrected.
